event: The War in Heaven / The Fall of the Rebel Angels
symbols: radiant white-robed angel at summit — Archangel Michael as commander of the heavenly host, clothed in divine righteousness
golden sword raised high — the word of God and divine justice executing judgment upon rebellion
falling muscular figures — the rebel angels cast down, their former beauty twisted by pride
red swirling vortex — the hellfire and chaos of damnation receiving the fallen
dark lower region — the abyss into which Satan and his angels are cast
kneeling figure at bottom right — a human soul witnessing or sheltered during the cosmic battle
descending diagonal composition — the downward trajectory of pride and rebellion against God
description: A monumental vertical composition depicting a violent cosmic battle between angels. At the top, a radiant angel in flowing white robes stands triumphant with a golden sword raised overhead, surrounded by a luminous glow. Below, a dramatic cascade of muscular nude and semi-nude figures — the rebel angels — tumble and twist downward in violent disarray, some wielding weapons, their bodies contorted in agony. A red swirling mass in the middle draws the fallen figures into darkness. At the bottom right, a cloaked human figure kneels in prayer or terror amid the devastation.
theological_description: Ivan Tvorozhnikov's depiction of the cosmic battle between good and evil spirits draws upon the apocalyptic vision of Revelation 12:7-9, where Michael and his angels wage war against the dragon and his angels. This primordial conflict — placed by tradition before or at the dawn of creation — establishes the fundamental biblical framework of spiritual warfare that runs from Genesis to Revelation. Michael's radiant triumph at the summit represents the assured victory of God's sovereignty over every form of rebellion; the sword he wields is both a literal instrument of divine justice and a figure of the Word of God that judges and divides (Hebrews 4:12). The falling angels, depicted with powerful physiques now contorted, embody the patristic teaching that sin disfigures even the most beautiful of creatures — Lucifer, the light-bearer, becomes Satan through pride. The human figure at the base reminds the viewer that this cosmic struggle has immediate personal consequences: every human soul is the battleground where these spiritual forces contend, as Paul teaches in Ephesians 6:12. The composition's vertical axis — from heavenly light to infernal darkness — is itself a theological statement about the direction of pride and the direction of grace.

scripture_refs.reason: Michael and his angels fighting against the dragon and his angels, who are cast down to earth — the direct subject
The fall of the morning star — 'How you are fallen from heaven, O Day Star' — traditionally applied to Satan's primordial rebellion
The struggle not against flesh and blood but against spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly places
Angels who did not stay within their own position of authority, kept in eternal chains under gloomy darkness
Jesus's declaration, 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven,' connecting the primordial event to His present victory

_meta.title: The Struggle of Good and Evil Spirits
